verses.text: Now these are they that came to David to Ziklag, while he yet kept himself close because of Saul the son of Kish: and they were among the mighty men, helpers of the war. They were armed with bows, and could use both the right hand and the left in hurling stones and shooting arrows out of a bow, even of Saul's brethren of Benjamin. The chief was Ahiezer, then Joash, the sons of Shemaah the Gibeathite; and Jeziel, and Pelet, the sons of Azmaveth; and Berachah, and Jehu the Antothite, And Ismaiah the Gibeonite, a mighty man among the thirty, and over the thirty; and Jeremiah, and Jahaziel, and Johanan, and Josabad the Gederathite, Eluzai, and Jerimoth, and Bealiah, and Shemariah, and Shephatiah the Haruphite, Elkanah, and Jesiah, and Azareel, and Joezer, and Jashobeam, the Korhites, And Joelah, and Zebadiah, the sons of Jeroham of Gedor. Guidance: The Chronicler tells of the men who came to David at Ziklag during his outlaw days, 'while he yet kept himself close because of Saul' — and remarkably, these were of Benjamin, 'Saul's brethren,' the very tribe of the reigning king. Skilled warriors who could use both right hand and left, they left their own tribe and king to cast their lot with the fugitive David. The chapter is showing us men of discernment and courage who recognized the LORD's anointed and joined him even when it was costly and dangerous to do so — when David was hunted and his cause looked hopeless. The chapter is teaching us the faith it takes to commit oneself to God's chosen one before his kingdom is established; these Benjamites believed the promise and threw in their lot with David in the day of his rejection, not waiting for the day of his glory. The chapter is teaching us that true allegiance to the LORD's king may mean breaking with our own kindred and the established powers when they oppose God's purpose — these men chose David over Saul, the LORD's anointed over their own tribe's claim. The chapter is teaching us that those who recognize and serve God's king in the day of his rejection share in the honour of his kingdom; they came to him in the cave and the wilderness, and the Chronicler records their names with honour. For the Christian, there is a clear application: blessed are those who commit themselves to Christ, great David's Son, now in the day of His rejection by the world, that they may reign with Him in the day of His glory.

And of the Gadites there separated themselves unto David into the hold to the wilderness men of might, and men of war fit for the battle, that could handle shield and buckler, whose faces were like the faces of lions, and were as swift as the roes upon the mountains; Ezer the first, Obadiah the second, Eliab the third, Mishmannah the fourth, Jeremiah the fifth, Attai the sixth, Eliel the seventh, Johanan the eighth, Elzabad the ninth, Jeremiah the tenth, Machbanai the eleventh. These were of the sons of Gad, captains of the host: one of the least was over an hundred, and the greatest over a thousand. These are they that went over Jordan in the first month, when it had overflown all his banks; and they put to flight all them of the valleys, both toward the east, and toward the west. Guidance: The Gadites who came to David are described in unforgettable terms — 'men of might... whose faces were like the faces of lions, and were as swift as the roes upon the mountains.' These warriors crossed the Jordan when it was in full flood, 'when it had overflown all his banks,' and routed their enemies on both sides. The chapter is showing us the kind of men the LORD gathered to His anointed: bold as lions, swift as deer, undeterred by a flooding river or a fortified enemy. The chapter is teaching us the marks of valiant servants of God — fierce courage ('faces like lions'), swift readiness ('swift as the roes'), and a refusal to let obstacles stop them (they crossed Jordan at flood). The faithful do not wait for the waters to subside; they go over when the LORD calls, though the river overflows its banks. The chapter is teaching us that nothing is too hard for those whom God has made strong and brave in His cause; the swollen Jordan that would daunt ordinary men was no barrier to the Gadites. The chapter is teaching us, too, that the LORD has servants of every rank — 'one of the least was over an hundred, and the greatest over a thousand' — and all are valiant, all are useful, each in his measure. The chapter is teaching us to be like these lion-faced, swift-footed men in the service of Christ: courageous, ready, and willing to press through every flood and danger to reach the King and fight His battles. The God who made the Gadites mighty makes His people strong for the work He gives them.

And of the Gadites there separated themselves unto David into the hold to the wilderness men of might, and men of war fit for the battle, that could handle shield and buckler, whose faces were like the faces of lions, and were as swift as the roes upon the mountains; Ezer the first, Obadiah the second, Eliab the third, Mishmannah the fourth, Jeremiah the fifth, Attai the sixth, Eliel the seventh, Johanan the eighth, Elzabad the ninth, Jeremiah the tenth, Machbanai the eleventh. These were of the sons of Gad, captains of the host: one of the least was over an hundred, and the greatest over a thousand. These are they that went over Jordan in the first month, when it had overflown all his banks; and they put to flight all them of the valleys, both toward the east, and toward the west. Guidance: The Gadites who came to David are described in unforgettable terms — 'men of might... whose faces were like the faces of lions, and were as swift as the roes upon the mountains.' These warriors crossed the Jordan when it was in full flood, 'when it had overflown all his banks,' and routed their enemies on both sides. The chapter is showing us the kind of men the LORD gathered to His anointed: bold as lions, swift as deer, undeterred by a flooding river or a fortified enemy. The chapter is teaching us the marks of valiant servants of God — fierce courage ('faces like lions'), swift readiness ('swift as the roes'), and a refusal to let obstacles stop them (they crossed Jordan at flood). The faithful do not wait for the waters to subside; they go over when the LORD calls, though the river overflows its banks. The chapter is teaching us that nothing is too hard for those whom God has made strong and brave in His cause; the swollen Jordan that would daunt ordinary men was no barrier to the Gadites. The chapter is teaching us, too, that the LORD has servants of every rank — 'one of the least was over an hundred, and the greatest over a thousand' — and all are valiant, all are useful, each in his measure. The chapter is teaching us to be like these lion-faced, swift-footed men in the service of Christ: courageous, ready, and willing to press through every flood and danger to reach the King and fight His battles. The God who made the Gadites mighty makes His people strong for the work He gives them.

And there came of the children of Benjamin and Judah to the hold unto David. And David went out to meet them, and answered and said unto them, If ye be come peaceably unto me to help me, mine heart shall be knit unto you: but if ye be come to betray me to mine enemies, seeing there is no wrong in mine hands, the God of our fathers look thereon, and rebuke it. Then the spirit came upon Amasai, who was chief of the captains, and he said, Thine are we, David, and on thy side, thou son of Jesse: peace, peace be unto thee, and peace be to thine helpers; for thy God helpeth thee. Then David received them, and made them captains of the band. Guidance: As men of Benjamin and Judah come to him, David, ever cautious in his outlaw days, tests them: if they come in peace to help, his heart will be knit to them; but if to betray him, he commits the matter to God — 'the God of our fathers look thereon, and rebuke it.' Then 'the spirit came upon Amasai,' and he answered with a ringing pledge of loyalty: 'Thine are we, David, and on thy side, thou son of Jesse: peace, peace be unto thee, and peace be to thine helpers; for thy God helpeth thee.' The chapter is showing us a Spirit-inspired declaration of wholehearted allegiance — the very model of how a believer should give himself to the LORD's anointed. The chapter is teaching us the beauty of total commitment: 'Thine are we, and on thy side' — no half-heartedness, no holding back, but a full and glad surrender to the king. The chapter is teaching us that the ground of such allegiance is the recognition that God is with him: 'for thy God helpeth thee.' Amasai cast in his lot with David not for gain but because he saw that the LORD's hand was upon David. The chapter is teaching us how to commit ourselves to Christ: 'Thine are we, and on thy side, thou Son of David' — recognizing that the Father helps Him, that His cause cannot fail, and giving ourselves wholly to Him. The chapter is teaching us, too, that David committed his cause and his safety to God ('the God of our fathers look thereon'), and that the LORD answered by sending him faithful men with willing hearts. The chapter is teaching us to entrust our cause to God and to receive gladly those whom He sends, and to be ourselves like Amasai — moved by the Spirit to declare, without reserve, our allegiance to the King whom God helps.

And there fell some of Manasseh to David, when he came with the Philistines against Saul to battle: but they helped them not: for the lords of the Philistines upon advisement sent him away, saying, He will fall to his master Saul to the jeopardy of our heads. As he went to Ziklag, there fell to him of Manasseh, Adnah, and Jozabad, and Jediael, and Michael, and Jozabad, and Elihu, and Zilthai, captains of the thousands that were of Manasseh. And they helped David against the band of the rovers: for they were all mighty men of valour, and were captains in the host. For at that time day by day there came to David to help him, until it was a great host, like the host of God. Guidance: More warriors fall to David — captains of Manasseh, mighty men of valour — and the Chronicler sums up the swelling tide of support with a striking phrase: 'day by day there came to David to help him, until it was a great host, like the host of God.' What began as a band of outcasts in a cave grew, by daily additions, into an army resembling the very armies of heaven. The chapter is showing us the LORD steadily building up His anointed, drawing men to David day after day until a mighty host had gathered. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's purposes advance not always by sudden leaps but often by faithful daily increase — 'day by day' the host grew, until it was great. So the kingdom of God grows, soul by soul, day by day, until it becomes a multitude no man can number. The chapter is teaching us to be encouraged in small beginnings; the great host 'like the host of God' began with a handful in the hold of Adullam. The chapter is teaching us that when the LORD is gathering a people to His king, He will not lack for soldiers; He moves hearts continually to come and help. The phrase 'like the host of God' lifts our eyes to the heavenly reality behind David's growing army — that the LORD of hosts was assembling His forces around His chosen one. The chapter is teaching us that those who join Christ's cause become part of a host that is truly 'the host of God,' growing daily as the Lord adds to His church such as should be saved, until the kingdom is filled and the King is glorified.

And these are the numbers of the bands that were ready armed to the war, and came to David to Hebron, to turn the kingdom of Saul to him, according to the word of the LORD. The children of Judah that bare shield and spear were six thousand and eight hundred, ready armed to the war. Of the children of Simeon, mighty men of valour for the war, seven thousand and one hundred. Of the children of Levi four thousand and six hundred. And Jehoiada was the leader of the Aaronites, and with him were three thousand and seven hundred; And Zadok, a young man mighty of valour, and of his father's house twenty and two captains. And of the children of Benjamin, the kindred of Saul, three thousand: for hitherto the greatest part of them had kept the ward of the house of Saul. And of the children of Ephraim twenty thousand and eight hundred, mighty men of valour, famous throughout the house of their fathers. And of the half tribe of Manasseh eighteen thousand, which were expressed by name, to come and make David king. And of the children of Issachar, which were men that had understanding of the times, to know what Israel ought to do; the heads of them were two hundred; and all their brethren were at their commandment. Of Zebulun, such as went forth to battle, expert in war, with all instruments of war, fifty thousand, which could keep rank: they were not of double heart. And of Naphtali a thousand captains, and with them with shield and spear thirty and seven thousand. And of the Danites expert in war twenty and eight thousand and six hundred. And of Asher, such as went forth to battle, expert in war, forty thousand. And on the other side of Jordan, of the Reubenites, and the Gadites, and the half tribe of Manasseh, with all manner of instruments of war for the battle, an hundred and twenty thousand. Guidance: The Chronicler now numbers the bands of every tribe that came to Hebron 'to turn the kingdom of Saul to him, according to the word of the LORD' — and here, at last, all twelve tribes are united around David. Two descriptions in this muster shine with spiritual wisdom. Of Issachar it is said they were 'men that had understanding of the times, to know what Israel ought to do' — men of discernment who read their day rightly and knew the wise course of action. Of Zebulun it is said they 'could keep rank: they were not of double heart' — disciplined, united, single-minded in their loyalty. The chapter is showing us the gathering of all Israel to God's chosen king, every tribe contributing its strength, even Benjamin (Saul's own kindred) at last coming over. The chapter is teaching us the great value of the men of Issachar's gift: understanding of the times. It is not enough to be strong or zealous; we need spiritual discernment to read our day and know what God's people ought to do in it. The church always needs such men — those who perceive the times and point the way of wisdom. The chapter is teaching us also the worth of Zebulun's spirit: undivided, disciplined loyalty, 'not of double heart.' The double-minded man is unstable in all his ways, but those who serve the LORD's king with a single heart, keeping rank, are a strength to His cause. The chapter is teaching us to be like Issachar in discernment and like Zebulun in wholehearted, ordered devotion — understanding our times and serving our King without a divided heart. And it is teaching us the glory of unity: all the tribes, with all their varied strengths, gathered as one to crown the man the LORD had chosen, according to His word.

It is not enough to be strong or zealous; we need spiritual discernment to read our day and know what God's people ought to do in it. The church always needs such men — those who perceive the times and point the way of wisdom. The chapter is teaching us also the worth of Zebulun's spirit: undivided, disciplined loyalty, 'not of double heart.' The double-minded man is unstable in all his ways, but those who serve the LORD's king with a single heart, keeping rank, are a strength to His cause. The chapter is teaching us to be like Issachar in discernment and like Zebulun in wholehearted, ordered devotion — understanding our times and serving our King without a divided heart. And it is teaching us the glory of unity: all the tribes, with all their varied strengths, gathered as one to crown the man the LORD had chosen, according to His word.

And these are the numbers of the bands that were ready armed to the war, and came to David to Hebron, to turn the kingdom of Saul to him, according to the word of the LORD. The children of Judah that bare shield and spear were six thousand and eight hundred, ready armed to the war. Of the children of Simeon, mighty men of valour for the war, seven thousand and one hundred. Of the children of Levi four thousand and six hundred. And Jehoiada was the leader of the Aaronites, and with him were three thousand and seven hundred; And Zadok, a young man mighty of valour, and of his father's house twenty and two captains. And of the children of Benjamin, the kindred of Saul, three thousand: for hitherto the greatest part of them had kept the ward of the house of Saul. And of the children of Ephraim twenty thousand and eight hundred, mighty men of valour, famous throughout the house of their fathers. And of the half tribe of Manasseh eighteen thousand, which were expressed by name, to come and make David king. And of the children of Issachar, which were men that had understanding of the times, to know what Israel ought to do; the heads of them were two hundred; and all their brethren were at their commandment. Of Zebulun, such as went forth to battle, expert in war, with all instruments of war, fifty thousand, which could keep rank: they were not of double heart. And of Naphtali a thousand captains, and with them with shield and spear thirty and seven thousand. And of the Danites expert in war twenty and eight thousand and six hundred. And of Asher, such as went forth to battle, expert in war, forty thousand. And on the other side of Jordan, of the Reubenites, and the Gadites, and the half tribe of Manasseh, with all manner of instruments of war for the battle, an hundred and twenty thousand. Guidance: The Chronicler now numbers the bands of every tribe that came to Hebron 'to turn the kingdom of Saul to him, according to the word of the LORD' — and here, at last, all twelve tribes are united around David. Two descriptions in this muster shine with spiritual wisdom. Of Issachar it is said they were 'men that had understanding of the times, to know what Israel ought to do' — men of discernment who read their day rightly and knew the wise course of action. Of Zebulun it is said they 'could keep rank: they were not of double heart' — disciplined, united, single-minded in their loyalty. The chapter is showing us the gathering of all Israel to God's chosen king, every tribe contributing its strength, even Benjamin (Saul's own kindred) at last coming over. The chapter is teaching us the great value of the men of Issachar's gift: understanding of the times. It is not enough to be strong or zealous; we need spiritual discernment to read our day and know what God's people ought to do in it. The church always needs such men — those who perceive the times and point the way of wisdom. The chapter is teaching us also the worth of Zebulun's spirit: undivided, disciplined loyalty, 'not of double heart.' The double-minded man is unstable in all his ways, but those who serve the LORD's king with a single heart, keeping rank, are a strength to His cause. The chapter is teaching us to be like Issachar in discernment and like Zebulun in wholehearted, ordered devotion — understanding our times and serving our King without a divided heart. And it is teaching us the glory of unity: all the tribes, with all their varied strengths, gathered as one to crown the man the LORD had chosen, according to His word.

All these men of war, that could keep rank, came with a perfect heart to Hebron, to make David king over all Israel: and all the rest also of Israel were of one heart to make David king. And there they were with David three days, eating and drinking: for their brethren had prepared for them. Moreover they that were nigh them, even unto Issachar and Zebulun and Naphtali, brought bread on asses, and on camels, and on mules, and on oxen, and meat, meal, cakes of figs, and bunches of raisins, and wine, and oil, and oxen, and sheep abundantly: for there was joy in Israel. Guidance: The chapter closes on a scene of glad unity and feasting: the men of war came 'with a perfect heart' to make David king, 'and all the rest also of Israel were of one heart' — and for three days they ate and drank together, with provisions flowing in abundantly even from the far northern tribes, 'for there was joy in Israel.' Here is the joyful coronation of God's chosen king by a united and wholehearted people. The chapter is showing us the blessedness that comes when God's people are 'of one heart' to crown the LORD's anointed; the long division and conflict of Saul's last years give way to unity, feasting, and gladness. The chapter is teaching us that wholeheartedness and unity are the soil in which joy grows — 'a perfect heart' and 'one heart' produced 'joy in Israel.' Where God's people are divided and double-minded, there is strife; but where they unite with full hearts around the King the LORD has given, there is feasting and joy. The chapter is teaching us the generosity that flows from glad hearts: tribes near and far sent food in abundance, sharing freely in the common celebration. The chapter is teaching us that there is deep joy in seeing the right King enthroned and the people of God united under Him. And it lifts our hearts to the greater joy yet to come — when all the people of God, gathered from every tribe and tongue with one perfect heart, shall celebrate the enthronement of great David's Son, and there shall be joy unending in the kingdom of our God. The crowning of David at Hebron, with its three days of feasting, is a foretaste of the marriage supper of the Lamb, when the King is honoured and His people rejoice with a perfect and undivided heart.
